About
Worksheets and reports

I Ching worksheets and reading report formats.

The free tool handles starter readings. This page keeps a clean path for printable worksheets, deeper reports, and email course products.

Worksheet

Question and Reflection Sheet

A printable sheet for question discipline, primary hexagram, changing lines, and follow-up notes.

Report

Hexagram Reading Report

A paid report structure for primary hexagram, relating hexagram, and movement interpretation.

Course

Beginner Reading Course

A lightweight email-course direction for users who want more than a one-time tool result.

Ask about report options

Report standard

What a paid I Ching report should include

A paid report should not simply repeat the hexagram name. It should explain the user's question, primary hexagram, changing lines, relating hexagram, decision context, practical next steps, and responsible boundaries in plain English.

Plain answer summary

The report begins with a short answer that names the main theme without promising a guaranteed result.

Changing-line guidance

Changing lines show where the reading is active. The report explains them as pressure points or adjustment points.

Scenario notes

Love, work, money, and timing notes help readers apply the same cast to common real-life contexts without overclaiming.

Delivery and boundaries

Reports are delivered as digital reading links after checkout confirmation. They are designed for personal reflection, journaling, and decision review. They are not emergency services, professional advice, investment advice, medical guidance, legal analysis, or guaranteed prediction. If a question involves harm, safety, legal exposure, medical symptoms, large financial risk, or another person who has not consented to pressure, use qualified help and real-world evidence before acting.

The free pages remain useful even without a paid report. Readers can use the coin tool, the 64-hexagram lookup, and the guide library to understand the structure of a reading before deciding whether a deeper report is worth purchasing.

Report examples

How to judge whether a report is useful

A useful I Ching report should make the reading easier to apply, not more mysterious. It should restate the question, identify the primary hexagram, explain changing lines, describe the relating hexagram, and turn the reading into specific next steps. If a report only repeats a hexagram name or promises a guaranteed outcome, it is not strong enough.

The current paid report format is designed for one cast and one question. It does not require a subscription. The reader receives a digital link by email after checkout confirmation. The report can be saved, printed, or reviewed later as a decision note. The value is in organization: a plain summary, the current pattern, the active pressure points, possible direction, practical scenario notes, and a short action plan.

Paid reports should remain clear about limits. They are not emergency support, therapy, legal advice, medical advice, investment advice, or proof that another person will behave in a certain way. They are structured cultural reflection tools.

Purchase clarity

Before buying a report

Before buying, the reader should already have one cast result and one clear question. The paid report is strongest when the input is specific. A question such as What should I pay attention to before changing jobs? gives the report a clear decision context. A vague question such as Tell me my future will produce a weaker reading because it has no practical anchor.

The report is a one-time digital product. It is meant for review, journaling, and decision planning. It can help a reader understand the cast in a more organized way, but it should not be treated as a personal oracle that removes uncertainty. The reader remains responsible for choosing what to do next.

The free sample and guide pages should make the product easier to judge. A visitor should understand what is included, how the report is delivered, and what the report cannot do before checkout.

I Ching Reading Guide

A calm I Ching tool for hexagrams, changing lines, and reflective decisions.
